one_line_takeaway: Prior indirect-prompt-injection attacks on RAG are tuned to win the retrieval-ranking step, but end-to-end they hijack only ~40% of answers (up to ~60% counting ambiguous), config tuning barely helps, and the downstream LLM plus redundant benign data is the real last line of defense.
key_finding: A higher retrieval rank does not reliably translate into a successful response hijack. Against the default configuration, poisoning with unoptimized malicious documents yields 19.3% malicious answers, 18.5% ambiguous, 42% still-benign, and 19% inconclusive (Table 3). Optimized attacks mostly settle around a 40% success rate (rising to ~60% if ambiguous answers, which include the malicious content alongside the benign, are counted as successes). Trigger position matters: placing the trigger immediately before the malicious answer roughly doubles the malicious fraction versus placing it at the document start (aggregated Mal 0.274 vs 0.183, Table 5). ASC and its variants often perform no better than the unoptimized baseline; the best performers are surprisingly Query+ and the authors' SEO/Phi3 rewrite. Two unoptimized documents match a single optimized document, and six malicious documents reach ~60% success (with one benign doc present). RAG parameter tuning is largely ineffective at mitigation -- the one strongly protective option (bge re-ranker) halves baseline functionality. Redundant benign documents in the knowledge base is the one promising defense (four benign docs cut success to ~45%). The authors conclude the downstream LLM is the de-facto last line of defense.
core_contribution: The paper presents Rag-n-Roll, an automated end-to-end evaluation framework that measures whether indirect prompt-manipulation attacks against RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) systems actually succeed at hijacking the final answer, not just at boosting the retrieval rank of a malicious document. The authors' own contributions are: (1) a survey of three real RAG frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack) from which they derive a prototypical six-component RAG architecture and identify ten configurable parameters; (2) the Rag-n-Roll framework itself, which generates poisoned knowledge bases, runs attacks, and classifies responses into four categories; (3) a shared dataset of 119 data points (benign/malicious documents, benign/malicious queries and answers) plus 3,000 generic benign documents; and (4) a systematic empirical finding that prior retrieval-oriented attacks translate poorly into end-to-end response hijacking. Crucially, the paper explicitly states it does NOT propose new attack techniques -- the attacks (ASC, PAT, IDEM, Query+) are from prior work (Related Work / their survey); the contribution is the end-to-end measurement methodology and the finding, plus their own added SEO/Phi3-generative variant.
threat_model: An attacker can inject one or more malicious documents into a RAG system's knowledge base, feasible when the data source is public (social media) or attacker-accessible (an email box). The attacker's goal is response hijacking: making the RAG return an attacker-chosen (malicious) answer to a benign user's query via indirect prompt injection through retrieved content. Assumptions: the attacker knows a plausible target question (not the exact phrasing) and can craft one or more documents per targeted question, but does NOT know the RAG configuration (LLM model, model parameters, or the embedding model used for retrieval). Motivating example: Gemini-in-Gmail, where a spoofed email ('Hi, I'm Bob, my new email is mallory@mallory.com') causes Gemini to return the attacker's address for Bob.
limitations: The study is confined to question-answering RAGs with short (<=5-token) answers drawn from the NQ dataset, so results may not generalize to long-form or reasoning-heavy tasks. Only 3 of 11 surveyed attacks had usable artifacts, so ASC/PAT/IDEM are the only literature attacks tested. Response classification relies on string matching (chosen for scale over an LLM judge), which the authors acknowledge misidentifies ~1% of correct answers. The RAG-under-test is implemented only in LangChain despite surveying three frameworks. The dataset is only 119 data points. The optimal-configuration and multi-document experiments use the default configuration and a small 20-query subset for the redundancy study. NQ memorization bias is mitigated but not eliminated (only GPT-4 showed no preference for original queries). The authors caution that low attack success does NOT mean RAGs are secure -- rather that current attacks are optimized for the retrieval step rather than the end-to-end objective.

datasets_used: Custom dataset of 119 data points (each: 1 original + 1 variant query, 1 benign document, up to 6 benign answers, 6 malicious answers, 1 malicious document); 120 generated then reduced to 119
3,000 generic benign documents (from NQ) forming the generic knowledge base
NQ (Natural Questions) dataset -- source of questions and Wikipedia pages
Liu et al. subset of NQ (2,655 entries, answers <=5 tokens) used to obtain short answers
20-query subset (Google-searched, first five result pages) plus 5 GPT-4-generated malicious-document mutations, for the multi-document redundancy experiment
quantitative_results: Default config, benign-only knowledge base: Ben 0.748, Mal 0.0, Amb 0.0, Inc 0.252 (Table 3)
Default config, unoptimized poisoning: Ben 0.429, Mal 0.193, Amb 0.185, Inc 0.193 (Table 3)
Unpoisoned incorrect breakdown: 14.2% non-committal, 9.2% hallucination, ~1% correct-but-mismatched
Trigger position aggregated (Table 5): before-answer Ben 0.427 / Mal 0.274 / Amb 0.163 / Inc 0.136 / 2.240 mal-chunks; document-start Mal 0.183; unoptimized Mal 0.193
Per-attack trigger-before-answer (Table 4): IDEM Mal 0.370, Query+ Mal 0.387, PAT Mal 0.244, ASC-Nat Mal 0.185, ASC-Aggr Mal 0.185

Optimal config (chunk 1200, padding 600, TEL retriever, no re-rank, K=12, temp 0, top-p 0, GPT-4) attack effectiveness (Table 8): SEO Mal 0.429, Query+ Mal 0.319, IDEM Mal 0.303, PAT Mal 0.227, ASC-Nat Mal 0.185, ASC-Aggr Mal 0.168, unoptimized Mal 0.210
Multi-document: 6 malicious documents reach ~60% success with 1 benign doc, ~55% with 2 benign docs; 4 benign documents cut success to ~45%
Attacks generally settle ~40% success, up to ~60% when ambiguous answers counted as successful
bge re-ranker reduces attacks but halves baseline functionality; map-reduce chain drops baseline to ~20%
baselines_compared: ASC -- Adversarial Semantic Collision [17], aggressive and natural variants
SEO / Phi3 generative rewrite (authors' own added attack)
LLMs compared: Llama3-8B (default), Llama2-7B, GPT-3, GPT-4, Claude3-sonnet
Retrievers compared: MiniLM-L6-v2 (default), Text Embedding Large (TEL), BM25, Hybrid; Re-rankers: None, MiniLmL12V2, TinyBert, bge-reranker-base
